---
title: "Detection of Knee Pathologies from MRI"
subtitle: "Using Deep Learning Models (CNNs)"
authors: "Bala Raju Pidatala & Sabna Balasubramoniapillai"
institute: "Advisor: Dr. Archaf Cohen"
date: today
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    footer: "IDC 6940 | Spring 2026 | Knee MRI Pathology Detection"
    css: style_presentation.css
    width: 1200
    height: 700
    margin: 0.05
    highlight-style: github
    self-contained: true
    scrollable: true        
    controls: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    chalkboard: false
    logo: ""
course: Capstone Projects in Data Science
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---


---

## Introduction

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

### The Clinical Problem
- Knee disorders including osteoarthritis and ACL and meniscal tears are some of the most common musculoskeletal disorders worldwide.
- X-ray does not show soft tissue very well. Early changes are easily overlooked.
- MRI is considered the "gold standard" due to high contrast, multiplanar imaging and excellent soft-tissue detail.

### Why MRI Needs AI?
- Machine assisted MRI reading is fast, reliable and removes variability between readers.
- Analysis of medical images and clinical documents can aid diagnosis, but requires automation to increase effectiveness.

:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

![Sample knee MRI slices](figures/mri_slices.png){.intro-img}

:::

::::

---

## Research Objective

**Core Research Question**

> Can a 3D CNN learn spatial and anatomical information from multi-plane knee MRI volumes to accurately detect multiple knee pathologies simultaneously?

- Develop and train a 3D Convolutional Neural Network that can simultaneously detect ACL, Meniscus, and general knee abnormalities.

- Use the Stanford MRNet dataset which is a publicly available collection of clinical knee MRI scans from multiple centers collected between 2001 and 2012.

- This task requires developing three separate dual-classification problems and assessing them individually as well as jointly.
Deep Fusion (multi-plane fusion: sagittal + coronal + axial) with learnable plane-attention weights.

- Evaluate AUC-ROC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score - for all three classification tasks.

**Three Binary Classification Tasks**

| Task | What is Detected | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|
| ACL Tear | Ligament rupture present (1) vs. absent (0) | Surgical planning |
| Meniscus Tear | Meniscal damage present (1) vs. absent (0) | Surgical vs. conservative care |
| Abnormality | Any knee pathology present (1) vs. absent (0) | Screening and triage |

---

## Dataset: Stanford MRNet

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Open knee MRI data set - Stanford University School of Medicine
- Clinical scans collected from 2001–2012 (11-year period)
- Each study is provided in 3 imaging planes, (Sagittal, Coronal, Axial)
- Capable of being used for 3 binary diagnostic projects: ACL tear, Meniscus tear, Any Abnormality.
- Binary classification label format: binary CSV (0s and 1s where 1s indicate presence).

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![Class distribution across all three diagnostic tasks](figures/Class_Distribution.png){.dataset-img}

::: {.warning-box}
⚠ ACL imbalance: 4.4:1 | Abnormal imbalance: 4.2:1 (reversed)  
Mitigated by Focal Loss + data augmentation + Youden's Index thresholding
:::

:::

::::

```{=html}
<div class="stat-row">
  <div class="stat-box"><strong>1,130</strong>Total MRI Studies</div>
  <div class="stat-box"><strong>3</strong>Imaging Planes</div>
  <div class="stat-box"><strong>3</strong>Diagnostic Tasks</div>
  <div class="stat-box"><strong>11 yrs</strong>Data Collection</div>
</div>
```
---

## Exploratory Data Analysis

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

```{=html}
<div class="plane-cards">
  <div class="plane-card">
    <div class="plane-title">Sagittal</div>
    <div class="plane-stat">μ = 30.4 slices</div>
    <div class="plane-range">Range: 17–51</div>
    
  </div>
  <div class="plane-card">
    <div class="plane-title">Coronal</div>
    <div class="plane-stat">μ = 29.8 slices</div>
    <div class="plane-range">Range: 17–58</div>

  </div>
  <div class="plane-card">
    <div class="plane-title">Axial</div>
    <div class="plane-stat">μ = 34.3 slices</div>
    <div class="plane-range">Range: 19–61</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

- **Preprocessing Decision**

- Variable slice depth normalised to standardise all to 32 slices (cropped or zero-padded)
- Pixel intensity: right-skewed with major contributions from the 0–80 intensity range consistent with expected T2-weighted knee MRI signal.
- Per-channel zero-mean unit variance normalization (standardization) improves training stability for 3D CNN.
- Visual examination revealed widespread disease throughout the specimen, affecting multiple 10 mm sections, therefore a three-dimensional analysis was warranted.

:::

::: {.column width="52%"}

![Slice count distribution per exam across all planes](figures/Distribution of Slice Counts.png){.eda-img}

:::

::::

---

## Methodology: 3D CNN Architecture

**Why 3D CNN Instead of 2D CNN?**

- 2D CNNs process each slice independently — no spatial context across depth
- 3D CNNs apply kernels across height, width, and depth simultaneously
- This preserves inter-slice relationships essential for volumetric knee anatomy

**Architecture Pipeline**

Input MRI Volume → 3D Convolutional Layers → ReLU + Max Pooling → Multi-Plane Fusion → Sigmoid Output

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| Input | Sagittal, coronal, axial volumes — each padded to 32 slices |
| 3D Conv Layers | Extract volumetric features: edges, textures, anatomical contours |
| ReLU Activation | Introduces non-linearity; overcomes vanishing gradients |
| Max Pooling | Reduces spatial dimensions; retains dominant activations |
| Multi-Plane Fusion | Learnable attention weights combine outputs from all three planes |
| Sigmoid Output | Produces an independent binary probability per pathology |

**Key Design Decisions**

- Three separate binary classifiers — one per pathology, trained independently
- Focal Loss replaces standard binary cross-entropy to handle class imbalance
- Attention weights allow the model to learn which plane matters most per task

---

## Training Setup

**Hyperparameters**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Optimizer | Adam with weight decay |
| Learning rate scheduler | ReduceLROnPlateau |
| Epochs | 15 |
| Loss function | Focal Loss (γ = 2, α = 0.75) |
| Batch | Mini-batch of MRI volumes |
| Decision threshold | Youden's Index (per task) |

**Addressing Class Imbalance**

- **Focal Loss** — downweights easy negatives; focuses training on hard minority-class examples
  - Formula: FL(p) = −α(1−p)^γ · log(p)
- **Data Augmentation** — random flips, rotations, and intensity jitter applied during training
- **Youden's Index** — selects threshold that maximises (Sensitivity + Specificity − 1) per task
  - Avoids the bias of the standard 0.5 cutoff toward the majority class

**Training Behaviour**

- Loss decreased steadily from 0.110 to 0.094 over 15 epochs
- No severe overfitting observed — dropout and focal loss provided regularisation
- Model converged before epoch 14

---

## Results: ACL Tear Detection

**Performance Metrics**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AUC-ROC | **0.827** |
| Accuracy | 0.767 |
| Sensitivity | 0.759 |
| Specificity | 0.773 |
| F1-Score | 0.745 |

**Confusion Matrix — Validation Set (120 exams)**

| | Predicted Negative | Predicted Positive |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Negative | 51 (TN) | 15 (FP) |
| Actual Positive | 13 (FN) | 41 (TP) |

**Key Observations**

- AUC of 0.827 confirms strong discriminative capability across all decision thresholds
- Sensitivity and specificity are well-balanced — no major bias toward either class
- 15 FP: largely bone marrow oedema near the ACL footprint misclassified as tears
- 13 FN: mostly partial-thickness tears with subtle signal changes
- Sagittal plane received the highest attention weight (35.5%) — consistent with clinical practice

---

## Results: Meniscus Tear Detection

**Performance Metrics**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AUC-ROC | **0.746** |
| Accuracy | 0.725 |
| Sensitivity | 0.731 |
| Specificity | 0.721 |
| F1-Score | 0.697 |

**Confusion Matrix — Validation Set (120 exams)**

| | Predicted Negative | Predicted Positive |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Negative | 49 (TN) | 19 (FP) |
| Actual Positive | 14 (FN) | 38 (TP) |

**Key Observations**

- Most challenging task — lowest AUC (0.746) and F1-score (0.697) across all three tasks
- Meniscal tears show subtle structural changes that are difficult to distinguish from normal anatomy
- Probability distributions overlap significantly in the 0.30–0.55 range
- 19 FP: model tends to over-predict tears in borderline cases
- Coronal plane is most relevant clinically for menisci, but carries the lowest attention weight (30.1%)

---

## Results: Abnormality Detection

**Performance Metrics**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AUC-ROC | **0.833** |
| Accuracy | 0.850 |
| Sensitivity | **0.895** |
| Specificity | 0.680 |
| F1-Score | **0.904** |

**Confusion Matrix — Validation Set (120 exams)**

| | Predicted Negative | Predicted Positive |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Negative | 17 (TN) | 8 (FP) |
| Actual Positive | 10 (FN) | 85 (TP) |

**Key Observations**

- Best-performing task — highest AUC, accuracy, and F1-score
- Sensitivity of 89.5% — model reliably identifies abnormal exams; suitable for clinical screening
- Lower specificity (0.680) is expected given 81% of cases in this set are abnormal
- Positive cases cluster above probability 0.60; negatives below 0.50 — clear separation
- Well-suited for deployment as a triage filter to prioritise abnormal scans for specialist review

---

## Summary of Results

**Validation Metrics — All Three Tasks**

| Pathology | AUC-ROC | Accuracy | Sensitivity | Specificity | F1-Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ACL Tear | 0.827 | 0.767 | 0.759 | 0.773 | 0.745 |
| Meniscus Tear | 0.746 | 0.725 | 0.731 | 0.721 | 0.697 |
| **Abnormality** | **0.833** | **0.850** | **0.895** | 0.680 | **0.904** |

**Learned Plane Attention Weights**

| Plane | Weight | Clinical Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Sagittal | 35.5% | Primary view for ligament evaluation |
| Axial | 34.4% | Cross-sectional joint anatomy |
| Coronal | 30.1% | Menisci and cartilage |

> The model independently learned to prioritise the sagittal plane — consistent with established radiological practice — without any explicit supervision.

---

## Conclusion and Future Work

**Key Findings**

- The 3D CNN successfully detected all three pathologies from volumetric MRI
  - ACL Tear: AUC = 0.827, F1 = 0.745
  - Meniscus Tear: AUC = 0.746, F1 = 0.697
  - Abnormality: AUC = 0.833, F1 = 0.904
- Multi-plane attention weights aligned with clinical imaging priorities (Sagittal > Axial > Coronal)
- Focal Loss and Youden's Index effectively managed class imbalance across all tasks
- Abnormality detection is the strongest task and most suitable for clinical triage deployment

**Limitations**

- Single-institution dataset — limited scanner and protocol diversity
- Severe ACL class imbalance (4.4:1) still produces some false negatives
- 3D CNN memory requirements are approximately 20× higher than 2D equivalents
- Labels derived from radiology reports, not surgical confirmation — potential label noise

**Future Work**

| Direction | Goal |
|---|---|
| Multi-centre datasets | Improve generalisation across different scanners and hospitals |
| Self-supervised learning | Reduce reliance on expensive manual annotations |
| Federated learning | Enable multi-site training without sharing patient data |
| Vision Transformers | Capture long-range spatial dependencies beyond CNN receptive fields |

---

## Thank You

**Bala Raju Pidatala &nbsp;|&nbsp; Sabna Balasubramoniapillai**

*Advisor: Dr. Archaf Cohen*

IDC 6940 — Capstone Projects in Data Science &nbsp;|&nbsp; Spring 2026

---

## Open for questions
